title: Cedar Root Basketry: Three Nation Traditions

steps.instruction: Learn cedar root harvesting: selecting young cedar trees, locating roots, and lifting carefully to preserve the root system.
steps.culturalContext: Young cedars (2-3 inch diameter) have roots close to the surface and at a workable size — typically 1-3 feet from the trunk, no deeper than 6-8 inches. The roots are carefully lifted with a digging stick rather than cut, preserving the live root system so the tree continues to grow. Roots are harvested in spring or early summer when the sap is running and the bark slips easily. Selected roots should be long, straight, and of consistent diameter — crooked or branched sections are avoided. The harvested roots are coiled into bundles and kept moist until processing.
steps.safetyNote: ANNOTATE -- root splitting: split cedar root away from body using bone awl or knife to initiate the split at the root end. Control the split by applying hand tension to both sides of the split root. Wet root splits more safely and evenly than dry root.

steps.instruction: Study Coast Salish imbricated basketry: coiled cedar root with colored overlays of beargrass, cattail, and cherry bark.
steps.culturalContext: Imbrication is a Coast Salish-specific decorative technique: strips of beargrass, cherry bark, or cattail are folded and wrapped around the coiling in a specific pattern to create the surface design. The technique name comes from the overlapping fish-scale (imbricated) appearance of the decoration. The coiling foundation is cedar root; the imbrication material sits on top of and is integrated with the coiling stitches. The geometric patterns in imbricated basketry are distinct to Coast Salish traditions — particularly those of Stó:lō and Musqueam weavers on the Lower Fraser River. These patterns must be attributed to the specific nation and community whose tradition is being studied.

steps.instruction: Study Nuu-chah-nulth plaited cedar bark baskets: flat cedar bark strips woven in a plaiting pattern.
steps.culturalContext: Nuu-chah-nulth basketry uses flat strips of cedar bark rather than round root — the bark is harvested from the inner layer of the cedar, processed into flat strips of consistent width, and woven in a checkerboard or twill plaiting pattern (over-under in two directions simultaneously). The resulting basket is flexible, functional, and well-adapted for carrying and storage in a marine environment. Plaiting is a fundamentally different technique from coiling — there is no coiled foundation, only interlaced strips. Nuu-chah-nulth basketry must be attributed to Nuu-chah-nulth peoples specifically; it should not be described as generic 'West Coast' or 'Northwest Coast' basketry.

steps.instruction: Study Nlaka'pamux (Thompson) coiled cedar root baskets: a third tradition with distinctive geometric overlay designs.
steps.culturalContext: Nlaka'pamux baskets use a fine cedar root coiling technique with distinctive geometric patterns — zigzags, diamond grids, and stepped designs — overlaid in beargrass or cherry bark. The Nlaka'pamux are an Interior Plateau people of the Thompson River watershed in what is now British Columbia. Their basketry tradition is related to but distinct from Coast Salish basketry — both use coiled cedar root, but the design grammar and overlay techniques differ. These patterns are associated with specific communities and must be attributed to Nlaka'pamux tradition specifically, not to generic 'Interior Plateau' or 'Northwest Coast' basketry.

steps.instruction: Study Ditidaht and Makah wrapped twining: a fourth tradition with whale and thunderbird imagery.
steps.culturalContext: Ditidaht and Makah wrapped twining produces a different surface texture than coiling or plaiting — the weft wraps around the warp strands rather than interlacing over-under, creating a distinctive raised pattern. This technique is well-suited to representing the whale and thunderbird imagery characteristic of Ditidaht and Makah design traditions. The Ditidaht are related to the Nuu-chah-nulth culturally and linguistically; the Makah are a closely related people of the Olympic Peninsula in what is now Washington State. These are distinct traditions of distinct nations, despite their cultural proximity.

steps.instruction: Compare the four traditions: understand why nation-specific attribution matters in basketry education.
steps.culturalContext: These four basketry traditions — Coast Salish imbrication (Stó:lō, Musqueam), Nuu-chah-nulth plaiting, Nlaka'pamux coiling, and Ditidaht/Makah wrapped twining — share cedar as a material but are distinct in technique, design grammar, and cultural origin. Each nation developed their basketry tradition in response to their specific ecology, material availability, and cultural values. Referring to any of them as simply 'Northwest Coast basketry' erases these distinctions. The technique shapes the form; the community shapes the meaning. When studying any of these traditions, identify the specific nation whose technique you are learning and attribute your knowledge to that nation throughout.
